quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Speciation"?

options.text: Small intracellular molecules (cAMP, Ca²⁺, IP₃) that relay and amplify signals from the receptor to target proteins
Insertion or deletion of nucleotides (not in multiples of 3) that shifts the reading frame, altering all downstream amino acids
The formation of new species through accumulation of reproductive isolation barriers; requires genetic divergence between populations
A 3-carbon sugar produced by the Calvin cycle; the direct product used to build glucose, amino acids, and fatty acids

quiz_questions.explanation: "Speciation" is defined as: The formation of new species through accumulation of reproductive isolation barriers; requires genetic divergence between populations. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Allopatric speciation"?

options.text: The enzyme that synthesizes ATP as H⁺ flows down its concentration gradient from the intermembrane space back to the matrix
Speciation driven by geographic isolation that prevents gene flow between populations, allowing independent evolution
A molecule resembling the substrate that competes for the active site; increases apparent Km but does not affect Vmax (can be overcome by more substrate)
A three-nucleotide sequence in mRNA that codes for a specific amino acid or stop signal; 64 total codons, 61 code for amino acids

quiz_questions.explanation: "Allopatric speciation" is defined as: Speciation driven by geographic isolation that prevents gene flow between populations, allowing independent evolution. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Sympatric speciation"?

options.text: 5' cap (7-methylguanosine) and 3' poly-A tail added for stability and export; introns spliced out by spliceosomes
A cluster of prokaryotic genes under control of a single promoter and operator, transcribed as one polycistronic mRNA (e.g., lac operon, trp operon)
Speciation occurring within the same geographic area, often through polyploidy (plants) or habitat/behavioral specialization
A 3-carbon sugar produced by the Calvin cycle; the direct product used to build glucose, amino acids, and fatty acids

quiz_questions.explanation: "Sympatric speciation" is defined as: Speciation occurring within the same geographic area, often through polyploidy (plants) or habitat/behavioral specialization. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Reproductive isolation"?

options.text: Multiple genes contribute to a single trait, producing continuous variation (e.g., skin color, height, intelligence)
Species with high reproductive rates, short lifespans, and little parental care (e.g., bacteria, insects); adapted to unstable environments
The enzyme that synthesizes ATP as H⁺ flows down its concentration gradient from the intermembrane space back to the matrix
Barriers preventing gene flow: prezygotic (habitat, temporal, behavioral, mechanical, gametic) and postzygotic (hybrid inviability, sterility)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Reproductive isolation" is defined as: Barriers preventing gene flow: prezygotic (habitat, temporal, behavioral, mechanical, gametic) and postzygotic (hybrid inviability, sterility). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Adaptive radiation"?

options.text: The monomer of nucleic acids: a 5-carbon sugar (deoxyribose or ribose), a phosphate group, and a nitrogenous base
A series of protein kinases that sequentially phosphorylate and activate each other, amplifying the signal at each step
Rapid diversification of a lineage into many species filling different ecological niches, often following a mass extinction or colonization of a new habitat
Insertion or deletion of nucleotides (not in multiples of 3) that shifts the reading frame, altering all downstream amino acids

quiz_questions.explanation: "Adaptive radiation" is defined as: Rapid diversification of a lineage into many species filling different ecological niches, often following a mass extinction or colonization of a new habitat. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The formation of new species through accumulation of reproductive isolation barriers; requires genetic divergence between populations"?

options.text: Final electron acceptor
Exponential growth
Speciation
Missense mutation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Speciation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Speciation driven by geographic isolation that prevents gene flow between populations, allowing independent evolution"?

options.text: Allopatric speciation
Nucleus
Missense mutation
Pre-mRNA processing

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Allopatric speciation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Speciation occurring within the same geographic area, often through polyploidy (plants) or habitat/behavioral specialization"?

options.text: Sympatric speciation
DNA polymerase III
Nonsense mutation
Free energy (ΔG)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Sympatric speciation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Barriers preventing gene flow: prezygotic (habitat, temporal, behavioral, mechanical, gametic) and postzygotic (hybrid inviability, sterility)"?

options.text: Molecular clock
Oxidative phosphorylation
r-selected species
Reproductive isolation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Reproductive isolation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Rapid diversification of a lineage into many species filling different ecological niches, often following a mass extinction or colonization of a new habitat"?

options.text: Adaptive radiation
Habitat destruction
Semiconservative replication
Tertiary structure

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Adaptive radiation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Macroevolution is accurate?

options.text: Macroevolution encompasses large-scale evolutionary patterns above the species level, including speciation, adaptive radiation, mass extinctions, and major transitions in body plan.
Minimum viable population (MVP), effective population size (Ne), and genetic rescue inform conservation management.
Biodiversity and Conservation addresses the variety of life at genetic, species, and ecosystem levels and the threats to it.
Biotechnology Applications encompasses the tools and techniques used to manipulate DNA.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Macroevolution as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Macroevolution?

options.text: Biotechnology Applications encompasses the tools and techniques used to manipulate DNA.
Environmental factors (temperature, pH, salt concentration) also affect enzyme activity.
Speciation occurs through geographic isolation (allopatric) or reproductive isolation within the same area (sympatric).
Mutation and Cancer explains how accumulated mutations in proto-oncogenes (gain-of-function to oncogenes) and tumor suppressor genes (loss-of-function) drive the multistep development of cancer.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Macroevolution as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Macroevolution?

options.text: The enzyme RuBisCO catalyzes carbon fixation (CO₂ + RuBP → 2 molecules of 3-PGA), making it the most abundant protein on Earth.
Alternative splicing enables one gene to produce multiple protein variants.
Adaptive radiation (rapid diversification from a single ancestor into multiple ecological niches) often follows mass extinctions or colonization of new environments.
Conservation Biology integrates ecology, genetics, and evolutionary biology to protect and restore biological diversity.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Macroevolution as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Macroevolution?

options.text: Macroevolution encompasses large-scale evolutionary patterns above the species level, including speciation, adaptive radiation, mass extinctions, and major transitions in body plan.
Environmental factors (temperature, pH, salt concentration) also affect enzyme activity.
Phylogenies reveal the nested hierarchy of life and enable predictions about uncharacterized organisms.
Saturated vs. unsaturated fatty acids differ in the presence of C=C double bonds, affecting fluidity and melting point.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Macroevolution as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Macroevolution?

options.text: Mutation and Cancer explains how accumulated mutations in proto-oncogenes (gain-of-function to oncogenes) and tumor suppressor genes (loss-of-function) drive the multistep development of cancer.
Protein denaturation (loss of 3D shape from heat, pH, or chemical changes) destroys function, demonstrating that structure determines function—a central theme in AP Biology.
Speciation occurs through geographic isolation (allopatric) or reproductive isolation within the same area (sympatric).

The fossil record shows transitional forms (Tiktaalik between fish and tetrapods), comparative anatomy reveals homologous structures (bat wing, whale flipper, human arm sharing the same bone pattern), molecular biology shows DNA/protein sequence...

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Macroevolution as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Macroevolution?

options.text: ABO blood type compatibility in transfusions determined by codominant Iᴬ and Iᴮ alleles and recessive i allele (3 alleles, 6 genotypes, 4 phenotypes)
Darwin's finches on the Galápagos Islands illustrating adaptive radiation: 14 species with different beak shapes evolved from a single ancestor to exploit different food sources
Aspirin acting as an irreversible inhibitor of cyclooxygenase (COX), blocking prostaglandin synthesis and reducing pain, inflammation, and blood clotting
Galápagos finch adaptive radiation: a single ancestor species diversifying into 14 species with specialized beak shapes for different food sources within ~1-2 million years

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Macroevolution.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Macroevolution?

options.text: Mass extinctions (e.g., K-Pg, 66 MYA) eliminating 75% of species but opening ecological niches that enabled mammalian diversification and eventual human evolution
Capillary action in plant xylem vessels transporting water against gravity from roots to leaves through cohesion-adhesion-tension
C4 and CAM photosynthesis as evolutionary adaptations to minimize photorespiration in hot, dry environments (corn, sugarcane, cacti, pineapple)
Prion diseases (mad cow, CJD) where misfolded proteins induce normal proteins to refold incorrectly, demonstrating the critical importance of protein conformation

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Macroevolution.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Macroevolution applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Lynch syndrome (hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer) caused by inherited mutations in mismatch repair genes, increasing cancer risk
Blood glucose regulation via insulin and glucagon controlling the interconversion of glucose and glycogen in liver and muscle cells
Whale evolution documented through a remarkable fossil series: Pakicetus (land mammal) → Ambulocetus (walking whale) → Dorudon (aquatic) → modern whales over ~50 million years
Ring species around geographic barriers (e.g., Ensatina salamanders around Central Valley of California) demonstrating speciation as a gradual geographic process

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Macroevolution.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Macroevolution?

options.text: Darwin's finch beak evolution on Daphne Major Island: drought years selected for larger beaks (to crack harder seeds), directly observed by Peter and Rosemary Grant over 40 years
Ice insulation of lakes in winter: because ice (density 0.917 g/cm³) floats, aquatic organisms survive below the frozen surface
Forensic genetics using Mendelian inheritance patterns to establish paternity or identify family relationships from DNA evidence
Galápagos finch adaptive radiation: a single ancestor species diversifying into 14 species with specialized beak shapes for different food sources within ~1-2 million years

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Macroevolution.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Macroevolution in practice?

options.text: Using molecular phylogenetics to trace the origin and spread of HIV, determining it crossed from chimpanzees to humans around 1920 in central Africa
Insulin signaling pathway: insulin binding to its receptor triggers a kinase cascade leading to glucose transporter (GLUT4) insertion into cell membranes
Mass extinctions (e.g., K-Pg, 66 MYA) eliminating 75% of species but opening ecological niches that enabled mammalian diversification and eventual human evolution
CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing using guide RNA complementary base pairing to target specific DNA sequences for precise genetic modification

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Macroevolution.
